name: Basic Pizza Dough
recipeInstructions:
1. Pour the warm water into a medium mixing bowl and sprinkle in the yeast. Stir gently with a fork until the yeast has dissolved and the liquid turns light beige in color.
2. Add 1 cup of flour and salt, then mix thoroughly with a wooden spoon. Add another cup of flour and mix well. The dough should start to come away from the sides of the bowl and form a soft, sticky mass. It is now ready for kneading.
3. Measure out the remaining flour. Sprinkle some over the work surface and flour your hands generously. Remove all the dough from the bowl and begin to work it by kneading in additional flour gradually.
4. To knead the dough, use the heel of your hand (or both hands) to push the dough across the floured work surface in one sweep. Clench the dough in your fist and twist and fold it over. Use a dough scraper to help gather the wet dough that sticks to the work surface into a ball while kneading.
5. Repeat this action, adding only as much flour as needed to keep the dough from sticking to your hands. Work quickly and don't be delicate. Slap and push the dough around to develop its gluten and facilitate rolling out (kneading pizza dough is a good way to relieve pent-up aggression).
6. When the dough no longer feels sticky, press the heel of your hand into it for 10 seconds. If there are clean hands, the dough is ready. If it sticks, a bit more kneading will be necessary. Continue kneading only until the dough is smooth and elastic (it should spring back when pressed) and shows no lines of raw flour.
7. Let the dough rise in lightly oiled 2-quart bowl, rolling the ball around to coat with a thin film of oil. Seal the bowl tightly with plastic wrap to trap moisture and heat from yeast's carbon dioxide gases, speeding up the rising process.
8. Place the bowl in a warm, draft-free location, preferably near an active gas oven pilot for electric ovens, set the thermostat at 200F for 10 minutes then turn off; this will provide sufficient warmth to raise the dough without cooking it.
9. Allow the dough to rise until doubled in bulk. Punch down by pressing your fist into the dough. Gases will release quickly and the dough will collapse. Remove from the bowl and knead again for about a minute.
10. The dough is now ready for shaping into a pizza or undergoing additional rising. Durable doughs made with bread flour, high-gluten, or semolina require an extra rise time of 15 minutes to 1 hour to develop gluten potential. This results in a more refined, even crumb structure and lighter-textured crust.
11. For second rising, add oil to the bowl and repeat the first rising procedure. The dough is now ready for shaping into pizza.
Pan Rising: Let the dough rise again in the pan for 10-20 minutes before baking for a slightly thicker crust.
Refrigerated Rising: If preparing the dough ahead of time, follow steps 1-6 and refrigerate overnight. In the morning, let it double, punch down, knead briefly, and allow to rise until ready to use that evening. Take the dough out of the refrigerator about 30 minutes before stretching or rolling.
